title: Term of the Day
description: Host introduces an AI/dev jargon term. Speakers are encouraged to use it naturally during their talks. Fun way to spread vocabulary.

examples.definition: Writing code primarily through natural language prompts, letting the AI handle implementation details.
The amount of text an LLM can process at once. Bigger window = more code it can see.
Software that acts autonomously, making decisions and taking actions without step-by-step human guidance.
Model Context Protocol. A standard for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources.
When untrusted input tricks an LLM into ignoring its instructions and doing something else.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Feeding an LLM relevant documents before asking it a question.
When an LLM confidently generates something that's factually wrong or made up.
Training a base model on your own data to specialize it for specific tasks.
The basic unit LLMs process. Roughly 3/4 of a word. Your bill is measured in these.
Hidden instructions that shape how an AI assistant behaves before the user says anything.
A project-level instruction file that Claude Code reads automatically to understand your codebase conventions.
The full setup around Claude Code: hooks, skills, MCP servers, and custom instructions that make it yours.
An isolated copy of your git repo that lets an agent work on a branch without touching your main checkout.
When an LLM calls external functions (search, code execution, APIs) instead of just generating text.
Self-contained content blocks (code, docs, diagrams) that Claude generates in a separate panel.

title: Quote of the Day
description: Host shares a quote from a builder, founder, or AI researcher. Brief reflection on what it means for the room.

examples.quote: The best way to predict the future is to build it.
examples.author: Alan Kay

examples.quote: We're not replacing developers. We're giving every developer superpowers.
examples.author: Dario Amodei

examples.quote: The hottest new programming language is English.
examples.author: Andrej Karpathy

examples.quote: AI won't replace you. A person using AI will.
examples.author: Unknown

examples.quote: Software is eating the world. AI is eating software.
examples.author: Jensen Huang

examples.quote: The best code is no code at all.
examples.author: Jeff Atwood

examples.quote: Ship it and iterate. Perfect is the enemy of done.
examples.author: Reid Hoffman

examples.quote: I'm not a great programmer. I'm a good programmer with great tools.
examples.author: Adapted from Brian Kernighan

examples.quote: Move fast and break things. Unless you're using AI, then move fast and let it fix things.
examples.author: Builder proverb

examples.quote: The future is already here. It's just not evenly distributed.
examples.author: William Gibson

title: Demo of the Day
description: Random audience member gets 60 seconds to show something on their laptop. No prep. Just pull up your terminal or browser and show what you've been working on.
examples: Show your Claude Code CLAUDE.md file
Show the last thing you shipped this week
Show the weirdest thing in your git history

title: Theme Setter
description: Host announces the theme of the night and gives a 1-2 minute framing. All lightning talks and table topics loosely connect to the theme.

examples.framing: Tonight we're talking about the automations, scripts, and AI setups that gave you time back. Not the flashy stuff - the boring stuff that quietly changed your day.

examples.theme: The Worst Thing Claude Ever Did
examples.framing: We've all been there. The deleted file. The wrong API call. The commit that broke prod. Tonight, we share our war stories.
How did you build software a year ago vs today? What changed? What stayed the same? Let's compare notes.
CLAUDE.md files, MCP servers, hooks, skills. Tonight is about the harness - how you've configured your AI to work the way you think.
One person, AI tools, production users. How are solo builders using AI to punch above their weight?
The prompts that shouldn't work but do. The workflows that feel like cheating. The moments where you thought, wait, that actually worked?
Custom instructions, few-shot examples, CLAUDE.md files. How do you teach an AI to think like you?
How fast can you go? Tonight we talk about building things in hours that used to take weeks.
AI-assisted debugging. When it's magical, when it's maddening, and what you've learned.
Contributing to open source with AI. Reading unfamiliar codebases. Understanding someone else's architecture in minutes.

title: Icebreaker Poll
description: Host asks a quick show-of-hands or live poll question to get the room engaged. Low-stakes, fun, reveals the crowd's composition.
examples: Raise your hand if Claude Code has ever deleted a file you needed.
Who here has spent more than $100 on API calls in one month?
Show of hands: who has a CLAUDE.md file longer than 100 lines?
Who has shipped something to production that was mostly AI-written?
Who here has tried to explain vibe coding to a non-technical person and failed?
Raise your hand if you've rage-quit an AI tool and come back the next day.
Who has more MCP servers than human friends? Just kidding. But seriously.
Show of hands: Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot, or something else?
